Alexander the Great Not So Great
TimelineTechnologyThe Philip Factorvs NapoleonSources

Alexander vs Napoleon

A comparison of two military leaders: one who built an empire from nothing, and one who inherited everything.

Category
Alexander
Napoleon
Army Creation
Inherited 30,000 professional soldiers trained by Philip II
Built the Grande Armee from scratch, creating corps system
Military Innovation
Used father's sarissa phalanx and combined arms tactics
Revolutionized warfare with corps system, independent divisions, and maneuver warfare
Empire Duration
Empire collapsed into civil war within 2 years of death
Legacy institutions survived; influence lasted decades
Administrative Skill
No stable government; focused on conquest only
Created lasting legal code (Napoleonic Code), reorganized education, infrastructure
Logistics
Relied on inherited supply system
Pioneered modern logistics and supply chains
Battlefield Tactics
Competent commander using proven tactics
Military genius; revolutionized tactics still studied today

By the Numbers

Years of Conquests

10

334-323 BC

15

1800-1815

Empire Duration After Death

2

Civil war

50

Lasting influence

Major Battles Won

8

Key victories

60

Won most battles

The Cost of Glory: Casualty Comparison

Alexander the Great

334-323 BC (10 Years)

Enemy Casualties

200,000+

Persians, Indians, and others killed in battle

Macedonian Losses

50,000+

Soldiers lost from inherited army

Result: Empire collapsed within 2 years of his death

Napoleon Bonaparte

1800-1815 (15 Years)

Enemy Casualties

1,000,000+

Across European campaigns

French Losses

400,000+

Grand Armée soldiers lost

Result: Napoleonic Code and reforms still influence law today

The Paradox of Casualties

Alexander: High Casualties, Immediate Collapse

Despite conquering vast territories and killing over 200,000 enemies, Alexander's empire was nothing more than a personal military dictatorship. Without him, it immediately fractured.

  • No administrative infrastructure
  • No succession plan
  • Empire split into warring successor kingdoms
  • All conquests lost within decades

Napoleon: More Casualties, Lasting Legacy

Napoleon caused far more casualties over a longer period, yet his impact endured. The Napoleonic Code remains the basis for legal systems in over 70 countries.

  • Created modern administrative systems
  • Established the Napoleonic Code
  • Reformed education and infrastructure
  • Influence persists 200+ years later

The Verdict: Casualties alone do not measure greatness. Napoleon killed more but built lasting institutions. Alexander killed hundreds of thousands yet created nothing that survived him.

Major Campaigns

Alexander's Campaigns

Persian Campaign (334-330 BC)

Inherited army conquers Persia using Philip's tactics. Victories: Granicus, Issus, Gaugamela.

Cost:50,000+ casualties
Result:Empire collapsed in 2 years after death

Indian Campaign (327-325 BC)

Forced march through harsh terrain. Costly victory at Hydaspes. Army mutinied at Hyphasis River.

Cost:Heavy losses from disease/desertion
Result:Abandoned, no lasting gain

Bactrian Campaign (329-327 BC)

Brutal guerrilla warfare against nomadic tribes. Alexander wounded multiple times.

Cost:High casualties
Result:Territory lost immediately after death

Napoleon's Campaigns

Italian Campaign (1796-1797)

Revolutionary tactics, outmaneuvered superior Austrian forces. Victories: Lodi, Arcole, Rivoli.

Cost:Minimal
Result:Established French dominance in Italy, lasting peace gains

Egyptian Campaign (1798-1801)

Scientific expedition + military conquest. Discovered Rosetta Stone.

Cost:Moderate
Result:Ended Mamluk rule, established French influence

Central European Campaign (1805-1807)

Austerlitz (1805) - "Battle of the Three Emperors" - masterpiece of maneuver warfare.

Cost:Significant but decisive
Result:Dissolved Holy Roman Empire, created Confederation of Rhine

The Verdict

While both Alexander and Napoleon were accomplished military leaders, the comparison reveals a crucial difference: Napoleon built his empire from scratch, revolutionizing military tactics and creating lasting institutions. Alexander, by contrast, inherited a fully functional war machine from his father Philip II.

Napoleon's empire survived his defeat and influenced Europe for generations. Alexander's empire collapsed almost immediately after his death, revealing his administrative failures and lack of lasting vision.
